topics.title: CBS Lawsuit and Contract Disputes
topics.summary: The studio details a legal battle with CBS over contract terms, allegations of secret payments, and the fallout from moving to satellite radio.

topics.title: Howard Stern and Les Moonves Legal Dispute
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal conflict between Howard Stern and Les Moonves regarding radio ownership, allegations of fraud, and the management of the radio division at CBS.

topics.title: Legal Dispute Over Radio Contract
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal ramifications and personal motivations behind a lawsuit filed against a former radio personality by his former employer.

topics.title: CBS Lawsuit Against Howard Stern
topics.summary: The show discusses the legal conflict between Howard Stern and CBS over his move to Sirius Satellite Radio, focusing on allegations of contract breach and the personal motivations behind the litigation.

topics.title: CBS Radio Lawsuit and American Idol Commentary
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal implications of a lawsuit against CBS radio executives while critiquing recent performances and judges on American Idol.

topics.title: Analysis of Recent Broadcast Interviews
topics.summary: The studio reviews recent guest appearances, discussing the dynamics between hosts and guests, specific revelations regarding personal relationships, and the impact of live call-ins on the show's flow.

topics.title: Joni Lauer Interview
topics.summary: The studio features Joni Lauer, a former professional wrestler who discusses her career trajectory, personal struggles with substance use following her departure from the wrestling industry, and her transition into new media projects.

topics.title: Joni Lauer Film Promotion and Personal Struggles
topics.summary: The studio features Joni Lauer discussing her role in an upcoming sci-fi film, her experiences with domestic abuse, and her personal journey toward sobriety.

topics.title: Joni Lauer Interview and Legal Discussion
topics.summary: The studio features an interview with Joni Lauer regarding her career, personal life, and upcoming film before transitioning into a discussion about legal disputes involving contract terms.

topics.title: Legal Disputes and Comedy Career Reflections
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion regarding a lawsuit involving satellite radio, the declining ratings of reality television shows like The Apprentice, and an interview with comedian Gilbert Gottfried about his career, personal life, and past performances.

topics.title: Gilbert Gottfried and Yago the Parrot
topics.summary: The studio features a caller recounting her interactions with Gilbert Gottfried and an interview with a woman named Wendy who claims to be speaking on behalf of a parrot named Yago.

topics.title: Red Peters Joins Howard 100 and 101
topics.summary: The studio welcomes Red Peters as a new host for the comedy music segment, featuring his parody songs and upcoming show debut.

topics.title: Gilbert Gottfried and Jane Fonda News
topics.summary: The studio discusses Gilbert Gottfried's record-breaking number of appearances on the show, his upcoming birthday, and reports regarding Jane Fonda's past relationships.

topics.title: News Segment on Crime and Celebrity
topics.summary: The studio discusses a variety of news stories including the arrest of a man for stalking children, Gary Glitter's trial in Vietnam, transgender identity issues in the workplace, and celebrity updates regarding Nick Lachey and Kate Moss.

topics.title: Drug Trends and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses the rise of methamphetamine as a major drug concern, the release of Gilbert Gottfried's DVD, and upcoming comedy performances.

transcript.text: Hey now, good morning everybody. And never a dull moment in my life. So yesterday I got
off the air to run over and do a press conference, which is really intimidating because you go
out and freaking 900 reporters waiting on what it is I'm going to say at my lawyer's
office. Oh, okay. So it wasn't even at like, uh, I mean, this time you really feel like
the president, you know, circus. Yeah. Like I, I summoned the press and everyone showed
up and I was nervous that I wouldn't be able to make all my points. You know, see, uh,
you know, I thought the news department was going to play the whole press conference,
but then I think everyone around here got nervous and told Liz, the news director not
to play it. Oh, really? For legal reasons. Yeah. And then, but it didn't make any sense
because Mel listened to it and thought it was great. Well, now they could play it then.
Maybe. Yeah. They should today at noon, but I mean, because I would love to hear the whole
thing. It was weird. It was even a little bit weirder than that because I guess, uh,
last night on the Howard Stern news at six o'clock, the first 15 minutes was supposed
to be about your whole story. And a Howard 100 news was prevented from doing it. So they
ended the newscast 15 minutes early. You're kidding. Oh, that's ridiculous. Uh, it doesn't
make sense. I think everyone around here was just being paranoid. Right. I don't mean the
news department. I mean everybody else. Yeah. You know, I, you know, I'm not going to harp
on it about this. Well, they named in this suit. Yeah. Yeah. Serious as to me, Don and
a serious. And you know, I went to less about this. I go, what are you doing? He goes, well,
you received a bonus. They said less the paper to the only paper. Here's your readers in
New York times. You only want to understand the deal. I didn't receive a bonus. It's called
an accelerated payment. I would have gotten the exact same money if one radio was sold
or 10 gazillion radios were sold as a matter of wind. So he goes, it was an accelerated
payment. I go, yeah. And he goes, well, that changes everything. I go, okay. So then what's
the problem? He goes, well, you have our tapes. I go, you guys can take care of the tapes.
I said, we both need a key to the archive. I've been spending a fortune archiving these
tapes. Anytime you want them, you can have them. I said, we both have, you know, we both
own them and I'm the only one who can use them because I have the intellectual property
rights. So I said, I'm not hiding anything from you. I said, I'm just trying to find
out from some of the company that you guys are going to archive these things in storm.
It's the only copies we have of the shows and no one will give me an answer. He goes,
okay, we'll work that out. So I leave thinking, okay, it's okay. And then two seconds later,
you're suing me because I received bonus payments. Now, anybody who knew that I was coming to
Sirius, regardless if you knew the exact specifics of my deal, no, I'm going to be paid. And
we know that this, this company is based on subscriptions. You know, Joel and Les give
me full. In fact, I'm going to play for you later. I can't play for you because it's,
it's from our old show. But lesson Joel came in, told me what a great job I did and how
they have serious radio that the whole thing is retarded and everybody on these newscasts
seem to understand that. But nevertheless, I got to spend a fortune in lawyers defending
myself against CBS and their lawsuit. Because a lawyer wouldn't see reason a lawyer sees
a payday. Yeah. Well, my lawyer said to me, you know, I know this is anybody who doesn't
feel weird when they get sued. Isn't, you know, something's wrong with them. He goes,
but you really should almost enjoy the publicity. Well, yeah, that's what you have to look at.
And this is going to put you back in the news and sell more radios. He said, but you know
what he goes, I'm telling you their case. There's no case. He says it has nothing to
do with your contract with serious. He said your contract with infinity allows you to
talk about anything you want. And they made record profits. They were sold out. You can't
be any more than sold out. Here's the harm. Well, they're saying that somehow I received
a bonus because of the sales of satellite radio. I sat down with less. I said, my deal
is public record. You can look it up right now. It's not a bonus. It's the same payment
I would have received. But what I'm saying is their lawyer wouldn't sit down with them
and say, look, you guys, I know you're hurt. No, this is coming from it. But it's time
to go. This is Steve Lehrman pumping Joel up and making them look like they're doing
something. The radio division's crumbling. I know what's going on here and I wish this
whole thing could have been avoided because it's a pain in my ass, but you want to know
what else has said into a beautiful relationship. This is a great 25 year or 20 year association
that benefited others much more than it benefited you. Yeah. And now it's forced me to do some
stuff and this will all be coming out in the month that I was going to let it go to the
grave with me, but I'm not gone anymore. I'm going to, I'm going to act on it. I'm going
to be in touch with certain people. I'm going to, I'm a fighter. I'm not going to let less
bully me. This is, you know, Les Moonves has always had it in for me. They head of a CBS
now and I'm going to tell you something. Les is a strange man. You, you, you sit down in
a meeting and I, and I asked Mel this question. He goes, Les a bright guy. And I'm not going
to tell you his answer because that's a private conversation, but I go, you know, I sit there
with him and I'm not sure he's comprehending what I'm saying. Like, I mean, literally doesn't
he, cause he started mumbling in this meeting. He goes to me, I'm damaged. You get, you damaged
me. I go, how he goes, my radio division isn't making any money right now. I go, Les, that's
not damages caused by me. Right. I'm allowed to leave. My contract was up. Yeah. It's the,
it's the weirdest argument ever. Well, I'm looking at this article here from, from a radio
records and they're sort of breaking it down. But the thing that's interesting that I see
that they're upset about, and I didn't, I didn't understand this. They say that you had it in
your contract, that you had to tell them that you were undertaking any other radio project
before you undertook it. I was on, listen, let me take that. I've heard that. So I, I
addressed that. I said on the radio, I'm going to talk, I'll tell you about my private conversations,
but I said on the radio, I don't want to be at CBS anymore. I don't want to be on terrestrial
radio anymore. I talked about satellite for five years. Joel came to me after Mel left,
he goes, do you want to do, you want to extend your deal? I looked him in the eye and I go,
Joel, I don't want to be on terrestrial radio FCC regulated. He did not believe me. He said,
oh, you're negotiating. You know, he chose not to believe me. I was going to say, is
it your fault? If you're not, I sat in a meeting and Joel said, I, Joel said, are you talking
to satellite? I said, yes. And he said to me, if you guys are talking to satellite,
let me negotiate the deal. I walked out of there with my agent and I go, what does he
mean by that? Why do we want him to negotiate? Does he want to leave Viacom and come with
us as part of this deal? I didn't even understand what he was talking about. That doesn't make
any sense for him to claim that he didn't know about this. This guy was saying, I want
to be in on the deal. And I didn't know if he wanted to be in on the deal and leave or
he wanted CBS to be part of it. I don't know what he was saying, but we had tons of discussion
about my plans. Well, how many people do you know? I mean, I'm not lying. How many people
do you know when they're looking for a job? Tell their bosses that I'm talking to this
one or talking to that one. Oh, you always have to. He knew I was in a different position
because he's a contract worker. But he fulfilled his contract. When you're under contract,
you do owe it to your. You know what they want? They want alimony from me. I said to
Les in the meeting. Les, I said, you just went through a divorce. You're like the ex-wife
that will not let go. Do you recognize yourself? He's supposed to be, don't I know what you're
talking about? I said, well, that's what you are to me. Right. He should recognize me.
The alimony analogy isn't right because alimony would be half. They don't want it all. Let
me tell you something. I firmly believe if I didn't mention a word about serious satellite
radio while we were at terrestrial radio, I didn't talk about it all. We had the same
amount of subscribers period. Those that were going to come with us were going to come with
us anyway. And people, P.S. are still coming with us. The fact of the matter is the fact
of the matter is, the reason Joel and Les put me on David Letterman and 60 Minutes,
the reason they allowed that is because when I talk about serious, it was a big story and
interesting and it gets ratings. The reason they let me on and fit me, they never once
said I was doing wrong except one time a month before I was leaving. For 14 months, they
said shit. And the reason they allowed that is because serious satellite radio was a big
story and they knew the ratings would be up. And I discussed this with Joel. I didn't
keep any of this. You think I could have gone on the air while Tom Chiusano and Joel had
their dumb buttons and done what I was doing? So then I heard one guy on TV going, "Well,
Les is a really smart guy." What he did was he let Howard put his foot in his mouth. He
let Howard go on there and knows he could sue him later. Well, you can't have it both
of you. You can't say, "Howard, it's okay to do something," and then sue me for it.
So what they're hanging their hat on is that I got a secret payment. They didn't know about
this bonus. It's not a bonus. Oh, but you were being paid by another company while...
Right. No, that I would get a bonus payment for subscriptions. I said it wasn't a bonus
payment. I would have received this payment later down the pike. It's the same exact...
They're claiming in the newspaper that my deal is even worth more money than they initially
thought. So what? It's not. Even if it was, but I'm telling you, that's not even correct.
They want both the money they made off you in advertising for the last year and a half
and the money you're making now. It's so unethical. They have to make a decision. My lawyers
said this is true. They made the money. Now you're gone. You're an American. You're allowed
to do whatever you want. Yeah, basically what they're saying is, "No, no, no. You're not
allowed to make money for anybody else." Right. The millions they put... What they're claiming
is, "Me being on their radio station, which I hijacked for 14 months and did whatever
I wanted to." Yeah, the two-minute delay and them suspending you whenever they want. Yeah.
My lawyer looked at it. I've hired two lawyers because I wanted two separate opinions. Each
one said to me, "You could just ignore this and let us just go deal with it." And chances
are that there's not even a... It's not even a legal argument. It goes back to your contract
with Infinity. It's just a new contract. You'll have to talk about whatever you want. Yeah.
I hate that you're even spending the money on the lawyers. It really makes me sick to
my stomach. Yeah. Well, I'll tell you what. It worked for them in a suit. It took off...
You know what? I was listening to one reporter. They were preparing a report that night on
how all the CBS radio stations are tanking. It took away... I told this in the press conference.
I wish I would play the whole press conference. It's a little boring. I'd love to hear it,
but I'm sure a lot of people would, but... I think they're going to play it on the news
today. Yeah. I'd love to do... Because then we get to listen to it, too. Yeah. I mean,
it's all the same points I'm making now. But, you know, Les Moonves, who has always had
it in for me... Why do you say that? Now that I don't understand. Well, we have a long history
of him feeling I was Mel's guy. And Les... Let me tell you about Les. Les is one of the
most vain... This is a guy when The New York Times puts him on the cover of a magazine
on their magazine section. I mean, this guy spends a lot of time staring at it. And I
got to tell you something. Everything to him. You know, he's not a businessman. He's a programming
guy. He's one of us. He's a talent. He's a failed actor. That's right. Well, while his
radio division is burning, you know what he's doing? He's putting himself on a remake of
network. Man, am I... You know, the company just posted a $9 billion loss, and wait till
you see the radio division losses. They're bleeding like crazy. And by the way, they're
going to blame David Lee Roth. They're going to blame Joel Hollinger. Les is going to come
out squeaky clean. Oh, yeah. And these guys haven't even had a month or two months to
get an audience. But you wait and see. Those guys are going to get screwed. They're going
to hire him. They hire him. They're going to screw Adam Carolla. They're going to screw
David Lee Roth. They're going to screw... I don't know Rover, but whoever he is, they're
going to screw him. They're not even going to give them a chance to get an audience.
But at least the very least you can say about David Lee Roth is that he made them work to
screw him. Yeah. You know, he has been a real royal pain over there. Oh, he's been away prick
over there, and I don't blame him. You know, it could hurt him. Because that's what they're
going to do. They're going to put all that crap in his lap. They hired a rock star. Yeah.
They hired the guy who broke up Van Halen because he's so miserable. Yeah. But you know, here's
the thing. So Les takes no personal responsibility. You should have heard him in this meeting I
have with him. He turned to Joel. I couldn't believe him right in front of me. He goes,
you really know how to pick radio programs. He started putting them down. So Joel's on
his way out. There's no question about it. And that's the sad part of this because Joel
and this other lawyer who works over there who will remain nameless concocted this whole
frigging lawsuit anyway. I know exactly what's going on. And Les is sitting there, and I
got to tell you something. Les is on top of the world. In his mind, he's got the number
one CBS network, which is his responsibility. He sits there. Like he thinks he's invincible.
There are so many son of bitches coming out of the woodwork who hate Les and are giving
me information. And I mean, some of it's outrageous. Really? Yeah. And the fact of the matter is
people hate this guy because he's sitting on top of the world and he's arrogant. His
network, but he's now in charge of more than the CBS television network. He's in charge
of radio and some other stuff. He wanted to step up. Yeah. He doesn't know a thing about
motivating a sales staff. He's going to blame Joel for the part. And by the way, Les is
in the battle of his CEO life. Tom Freston and Les are two guys battling to take over
control when some of the redstone leaves or dies. It's now a battle of who's going to
win. Freston sitting back with a big smile on his face. Les looks like an out of control
jackass right now. This makes Freston look like a guy who's got it all together. Yeah.
It almost reminds you of the fall that Michael Eisner took. Yeah. You know, he put himself
out there, made himself a big name and then he got all of his public failings were seen
by everyone. So Les is sitting there and Joel and this lawyer come to him with a cockamamie
lawsuit and Les is on top of the world. He's going to teach Howard Stern. And if you want
to go into why he has a hatred of me, I ridiculed Les for a really long time. Les is a very
vain guy. He doesn't like anyone. I'm not like David Letterman making fun of Les. I'm
talking about I really got down and dirty about Les. Les, in my opinion, screwed me
over on a television deal that I had lined up and he didn't like that Mel was keeping
me on television. There's a long history between me and Les of problems. And now I'm just giving
you some memories. So, you know, Les is a guy now, a CEO who's sitting on top of the
world. He thinks because his stupid television network is number one. Everything else is
falling around him. You still got to make money. You still got to make money at the
end of the day. Preston's completely in control of his thing. He's not sitting and getting
dumb headlines. And this is bad. You know, I was reading a guy's column, an industry
column. I'll read it to you. The guy doesn't even like me as a performer. You can tell
he's knocking me all over the place. He goes, but what is Les Moonves doing? This guy is
saying the suit has no merit, number one. You can't have Howard Stern on the air and
at the same time give him permission and at the same time sue him. But here's what the
guy said. Why is Les Moonves, everyone has forgotten about Howard Stern, he goes. This
is his article. He goes, he's on Sirius satellite radio. He doesn't have a big audience anymore.
Let him die. Don't you just put him back on the front page and on every television station.
Yeah, it's a major blunder. The guy goes, Howard made it on self promotion, off time
shameless self promotion. And that's the other thing. I talk about everything in my life.
That's what I was seeing at the press conference. Talking about Sirius, I talked about my books
and my movies on Viacom. They didn't participate in the profits. And of course I made profits
from that book and from the movie. I don't have to spell it out for you, do I? Howard
made it on self promotion. Howard creates a train wreck. The media covers it. People
tune into his show and money is made. He said the act was getting old. Howard got divorced.
People had seen the movie. Howard did not dominate in every market like he once did.
Bullshit. We were thrown out of everything. No kidding. And then he says, and here's
the dirty little secret. Howard is ensconced at Sirius and nobody's listening. Compared
to what he used to, he has three million listeners and there's over a hundred channels. He's
saying the guy is off doing a little dog and pony show and what does less do? He goes and
sues Howard Stern and puts him back on the front page of the newspaper. This is the guy's
column. Less Moonves is knowledgeable about television but his potential lawsuit regarding
Stern makes me wonder about his insight into radio. His old cohort now running the other
side of Viacom, Tom Freston, would never do this. You never want to alienate talent, especially
when they have other options. You want to woo talent. Sharon Osborn fucked with MTV
in a way nobody else ever has. Did you see Tom Carping in the press that Sharon has never
signed the original contract and screwed him? No. What is this message? I've said it to
some of the jocks I know still working over there. I said, "Less, we'll screw you. You
watch what they do to David Lee Roth, Adam Carolla and this guy Rover. Watch." That's
not giving shit about talent. Guy even says, "It's not too late, Less. Howard's just begun.
The streets are ready for giving you for CBS Radio's poor performance. Use this to rebuild.
Come up with something out of left field like you did with Survivor. Take a chance. Do something
different. Pay attention to the radio division. Don't sit with Sue Howard." As for Howard,
if you're not listening to Sirius, you've got no idea how good he is. He's as good if
not better than ever. But almost nobody knows this. Almost nobody talks about his show anymore
because as stated earlier, nobody hears it. Now here's a guy who hated me. He's listening
to me on Sirius and saying I'm better than ever, but you know. Forget about it. But forget
about it. Don't even pay attention. You know. If CBS just said it wished Howard good luck
and focused all of its press on its new talent, there'd be no story. The funeral would die
down. They'd have room to breathe. CBS would have the advantage. And now they've squandered
it. In the spirit of full disclosure, I have a radio show on KLSX, which is ultimately
Less Moon by Sonny's paycheck. And even this guy is saying, "Less, what are you doing?"
You know, it was mind-blowing. I sat in a meeting with him. I said, "What are you doing?
Say goodbye. It's over." So you know, I've got to defend the thing. And you know, listen,
as calm as you can be about a lawsuit.
When I heard the amount they were suing you for, I was like, "Are they out of their mind?"
I think if you add up all the advertising dollars, let's say I bought advertising. I
don't even think you could come up with that much money. It's ridiculous. I mean, it's
unbelievable. How could you put me on David Letterman in 60 minutes?
And be upset with you at the same time.
Yeah. How do you come on my show the last week and say you are an owner of Sirius Satellite
Radio and you're going to be listening every morning and that you did a good job. Congratulations,
you did a good job.
Unbelievable. I mean, please understand and I always run the risk with my audience of
harping on stuff. So if you don't want me to talk about this, I won't. But I think it's
fascinating and they really are trying to hide the fact, did you see the ratings? Did
you see? Forget about David Lee Roth and Adam Carolla and Rover. They need time to build.
Yeah, that's one month for them.
But if I was that important to the company, how did Les Moonves let me leave? He's embarrassed.
Well, that was the whole point. Nobody was ever treating you like you were a superstar,
that you were somebody they needed to keep.
Les never walked in once and said to me, hey, I know you're getting hammered by the FCC.
I know we're running 21 minutes of commercials. Here's my plan for you. Here's what we're
going to do. And by the way, I wasn't interested. Anyway, I told that to Joel for them to claim
that they didn't know that I was. I have discussed my future with Joel many times.
Joel didn't like the answer.
Even the few negotiations you had with them weren't all that pleasant in the last couple
of years, you know, when they were putting us back on radio stations when we got kicked
off by Clear Channel. I don't think that went very well.
Telling me that I wasn't worth the same amount of money, because no one else will touch me.
No, I never discussed my future with them. I love that. And Kevin, you're on the air.
Hey, Howard, I wanted to ask you if you heard that the Chicago Bulls are still in Michael
Jordan, because they're teen fucks and they can't win the answer.
Well, that's what this is.
It's about the same thing, yeah.
The only scary part in this for me is that, you know, I just didn't want--everyone said
to me, well, why did you go to the press yesterday? Why did you hold a press conference before
they even filed papers? I said, well, I knew they were filing papers. We'd been informed.
I said, there's something that really bothers me. The one thing that bothers me, what lawyers
do is they stick in these papers, fraud, words like that. Howard Stern committed fraud. Howard
Stern is keeping our property from us. They're referring to the tapes, which I've had a million
discussions with them about. I still can't get an answer from them, what I'm supposed
to be doing with them. Then fraud, theft. And you know what? Then the headline is Howard
Stern accused of fraud. I didn't want that. You know what? I didn't care. I said, I'm
going to go to the press and tell them what's going on here, because I really just don't
want my name. All I've got is my reputation. Honest to God, you don't have anything else
in life. You are--what you are in life is a series of moves that you make, and people
can evaluate that. And I'm pretty evaluated. I put myself out there to be evaluated. And
the thing I can hang my hat on is my reputation. If Joel had ever said to me, don't mention
satellite. No discussion period. That's the way it would have been. He was in on it. Less
was in on it. They encouraged me. On several occasions, Joel told me he thought it was
really funny. He liked the way I was handling it. After he was suspended me, we had a meeting.
I called him, or he called me. I don't remember. But we both had a conversation. He goes, I
like what you're doing. It's fine. Now look, you can't have it both ways. You can't turn
around and sue me. Right. They had already decided that they were going to sue you while
they were saying that. Yeah. It's just such BS. And I know what this is. It's sour grapes.
And Les kept saying over and over again in this meeting, it's not personal. It's business.
It's not personal. It's business. And I pointed out this in the press conference. When a guy
says it once, he'd believe him. When he says it 50 times, it's personal. When a guy turns
to you in a meeting and goes, and I had to learn about you leaving CBS on my birthday.
And I went, what is this guy, 10 years old? Hey, Les, it's not personal. It's business.
Lisa G came in here and said yesterday, what is he, 10 years old? I mean, you get the same
reaction. But I mean, that's a very personal response. It was my birthday. It's all personal.
This lawsuit has nothing to do with anything but that. It's a personal, they are instead
of concentrating on the radio division, which they don't have a clue what to do with it.
You know what they ought to do? Drop the lawsuit. Let me run the radio division. I'll have that
thing up and running in a week. Joe Hollander doesn't know what to do with the radio division
and Les Moonves sure as hell doesn't know what to do. And you're going to see. You're
going to see. Les, it's going to kill them. Not what to do. They don't know who to talk
to. Yep. And if you sit in a meeting with Les and I explain to him, Les, here's my deal.
I would receive the same payments whether you sold one radio or 50. And he goes, well,
how is that so? And I tell him, accelerate. He goes, you know what accelerated payment
was? He goes, oh, well, if that's the case, then that's different. But he gets back in
with the lawyers and they work them out. And quite frankly, he says one thing in the meeting
and then just goes back and does another. And I really, you know, my lawyer seems to
feel very confident that this thing will be dismissed. Maybe it won't, you know, because
the celebrities attached to it. Who knows? But I think ultimately when it all washes
out, it'll be, it'll, they claim it'll prove to be nothing, but you've got to take it seriously.
Yeah, but it'll be interesting, you know, to see these lawyers present this case to
a judge. Yeah. Well, you know, the, the, yeah, you know, it's just unfortunate and it's sad.
It's sad because these guys can't move on. I've moved on. I don't even spend a minute
thinking about terrestrial radio anymore. I love being here and it's driving them nuts.
I'm out of that. And it's a lot of envy on their part. I mean, you know, Les thinks
he's the top honcho. He should be making all the dough. I think it's freaking him out.
